nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone (the near-shore, boulder-strewn shallows) of Lake Malawi, endemic to Pombo Rocks off the Tanzanian coast. Found living and feeding close to the rock surfaces and within crevices.

decorPreferences: Heavy rockwork with many caves and crevices
Open swimming space between rock structures
Minimal or no plants (will be uprooted)

diet.notes: Primarily vegetable matter — spirulina flakes, blanched spinach, nori, and quality Mbuna pellets are generally advised. Avoid meaty foods (red meat, bloodworms) as these have been associated with bloat (Malawi bloat), which can be fatal. Occasional live or frozen invertebrates in small amounts are sometimes offered but meaty protein should be kept to a minimum.

goodWith: Robust mbuna of similar size and different coloration/pattern (e.g. Labidochromis caeruleus, Cynotilapia afra, Metriaclima species)
Other Lake Malawi cichlids with comparable aggression levels
avoidWith: Conspecifics in groups smaller than 10–12 (aggression concentrates on individuals)

imageCandidates.notes: Emoke Denes; 3934x2622; sharp high-res group of multiple individuals showing the electric-blue-and-black vertical bars, well-suited to this shoaling mbuna.
Mario Rubio Garcia; 800x536; crisp full-body side profile of a single vibrant fish with classic alternating electric-blue and black bars.
Lee Nachtigal; 935x584; sharp lone specimen in natural rocky biotope, strong electric-blue-and-black barring.

summary: The Demasoni Cichlid is a small, electric-blue-and-black-barred Mbuna from Pombo Rocks, Lake Malawi, widely regarded as one of the most aggressive cichlids for its size. Keeping 12 or more individuals is generally advised to spread aggression across the group.
careNotes:
The Demasoni Cichlid, known scientifically as Chindongo demasoni (the older name Pseudotropheus demasoni is still common in the aquarium trade), is widely regarded as one of the most aggressive Mbuna in the hobby despite reaching only around 7–8 cm as an adult. The central challenge is group dynamics: in small groups of fewer than around ten fish, aggression concentrates relentlessly on a handful of subordinate individuals, which are frequently harassed to death. Keeping 12 or more specimens — ideally in a species-only setup or alongside other robust Mbuna — tends to spread aggression enough that no single fish bears the brunt. A 200-litre aquarium is the absolute minimum floor for a group of this size; 300 litres or more is generally preferred for long-term success.

Sexing is extremely difficult, as males and females appear virtually identical in coloration and pattern as adults, which complicates planning stocking ratios. A captive-bred long-fin morph exists and is occasionally available in the hobby.

The Demasoni Cichlid is a strict herbivore, and a protein-heavy diet has been associated with Malawi bloat, a potentially fatal digestive condition. Meaty foods such as bloodworms and red meat should be avoided or used only in trace amounts.

Water must be hard and alkaline to match Rift Lake conditions, and the species tends to fare poorly in acidic or soft water. Tank décor should replicate the rocky shoreline biotope with extensive cave structures. The fish have been observed swimming upside-down along rock surfaces — a normal feeding and territorial behavior, not a sign of illness.

IUCN Red List status: Vulnerable, assessed 2018. The species is endemic to Pombo Rocks, Tanzania, giving it an exceptionally restricted natural range.
breedingNotes: The Demasoni Cichlid is a maternal mouthbrooder, meaning the female carries fertilized eggs inside her mouth until they hatch and the fry are ready to be released. Spawning typically occurs over flat rock surfaces, and females incubate the eggs for approximately three weeks before releasing free-swimming fry. The fry generally accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and powdered spirulina foods from the start. Breeding difficulty is generally considered manageable once a colony is established — the main challenge is identifying the sex of individuals, since males and females look nearly identical, which makes building a female-heavy ratio difficult. Starting with a species-only tank and 6–8 young fish of mixed sex is a commonly recommended approach.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters (size, water parameters, tank dimensions, diet, temperament, breeding). Note: page still listed under former name Pseudotropheus demasoni; current valid name is Chindongo demasoni per FishBase 2026.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed current valid scientific name Chindongo demasoni (Konings, 1994); family Cichlidae, order Cichliformes; max SL 6.3 cm; native range Lake Malawi, Tanzania. IUCN Vulnerable status noted.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; females carry fertilized eggs in the mouth for approximately three weeks before releasing free-swimming fry. Sexing is difficult, as males and females are nearly identical in coloration and pattern. Breeding is generally considered manageable once a colony is established.
